title_en: Social Ethics and Legal Rulings in the Commentary
title_ar: الأخلاق الاجتماعية والأحكام الفقهية في الشرح
content_en:
The social ethics and legal rulings sections of Ikmal al-Mu'lim cover the full range of Sahih Muslim's chapters on commerce, family relations, criminal law, and social conduct with the thoroughness that characterizes Qadi Iyad's approach throughout the work. The commercial law discussions are particularly important for understanding the Maliki doctrine of istislah — the use of public interest considerations as a source of legal rulings — applied to the commercial traditions of Sahih Muslim. Qadi Iyad's articulation of this doctrine in the context of specific commercial hadiths is one of the clearest available in the classical Maliki literature.

The family law sections address the traditions on marriage, divorce, and custody with the same linguistic precision and Maliki juristic analysis that characterizes the worship sections. Qadi Iyad's discussions of the traditions on divorce are particularly significant, since the Maliki school held distinctive positions on the irrevocability of certain pronouncements of divorce and on the conditions governing the various forms of dissolution of marriage. His arguments for the Maliki positions with reference to the Sahih Muslim traditions are important primary sources for the history of Maliki family law.

The sections on governance, justice, and the obligations of rulers draw on Sahih Muslim's significant coverage of political ethics and governance to articulate the Maliki understanding of Islamic political life. Qadi Iyad, who served as a judge under multiple rulers and experienced the political turbulence of the Andalusian and Almohad periods, brought a practical seriousness to these discussions that scholars who worked only in academic contexts often lack. His understanding of the pressures and dilemmas of judicial practice under political constraint is reflected in his nuanced discussions of these politically sensitive topics.

The sections on eschatology and the afterlife — covering Sahih Muslim's extensive chapters on the signs of the Last Hour, the Day of Judgment, paradise, and hellfire — receive from Qadi Iyad treatment that integrates Ash'ari theological analysis with careful linguistic commentary. His discussions of how to understand the vivid eschatological descriptions in the hadiths in a way consistent with divine transcendence are important contributions to the theology of the afterlife in the classical Islamic tradition.
